quotes.verbatim_quote:
There are only three Diminished Scales. The five examples

1

are all to be played against Dominant 7th Chords. Find the
chords beyond the B7 given.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Foundational rule: three diminished scales, exclusively over Dominant 7th chords.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
There are but two Whole Tone Scales. They will fit with all
Dominant 7 +5 Chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defining rule for whole tone application — only two scales, only over Dominant 7+5.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The melodic/scale patterns, in the following examples, grow out of the chords at the beginning of each line, which should be apparent when they are played.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Core compositional principle: lines are chord-derived, not scale-first.

quotes.verbatim_quote: It may help the student to develop his own ideas by first putting the changes on tape and playing lines against them or having someone else play the changes with the student.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Rhythm-section simulation via tape or partner for internalizing chord-tone improvisation.

quotes.verbatim_quote: These changes should be played out of tempo so that the student can establish a long line and fully realize the potential richness of each chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Removing tempo constraint to explore full melodic depth of each chord during learning.
